questions.question: Choose the correct verb form after the preposition.
questions.explanation: When a verb comes after a preposition like "for," we use the gerund form (verb + ing). Therefore, "making" is correct.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: The expression "think of" is followed by a gerund. After prepositions, we use verb + ing.

questions.question: Choose the correct form after the adjective.
questions.explanation: Some adjectives are followed by specific prepositions. "Tired of" must be followed by a gerund (waiting).

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: "In spite of" is followed by a noun or a gerund, not a full clause. "The rain" is a noun phrase.

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence.

prompt.text: _____ he was nervous, he gave a great speech.

questions.explanation: "Although" must be followed by a clause (subject + verb). "He was nervous" is a complete clause.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: "Even though" is followed by a complete clause (subject + verb). "She was tired" is grammatically correct.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb form.

prompt.text: I will message you as soon as I

questions.explanation: After time expressions like "as soon as," we use the present tense to talk about the future, not "will."

questions.question: Choose the correct verb form.
questions.explanation: With time conjunctions like "when," we use the present simple to refer to future time.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: We use "so + adjective + that" to express result. "So difficult that" shows the consequence.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.

prompt.text: students in the hall that it was crowded.

questions.explanation: "Students" is a countable plural noun, so we use "many" with it.

questions.question: Choose the correct form.
questions.explanation: After "used to," we use the base form of the verb (wake), not the past or -ing form.

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: When we were kids, we would visit our grandparents every Friday.
When we were kids, we would have a small house.
When we were kids, we would be shy.
When we were kids, we would know many people.
questions.answer: When we were kids, we would visit our grandparents every Friday.
questions.explanation: "Would" is used for repeated past actions (habits), not for past states like "have" or "be."

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: "Message" is singular, so we use "There is" with singular nouns.

questions.arabicHint: جمع كلمة تنتهي بـ -y (حرف ساكن قبلها)
questions.question: Choose the correct plural form.
questions.explanation: If a noun ends in a consonant + y, we drop the "y" and add "-ies" to form the plural.

questions.question: Choose the correct article.
questions.explanation: We use "the" with things that are unique or one of a kind, such as the sun.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb form after the preposition.
questions.explanation: After a preposition like "on," we must use a gerund (verb + ing). Therefore, "paying" is correct.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: The verb "succeed in" is followed by a gerund. We say "succeeded in passing," not "to pass."

questions.question: Choose the correct question form.

prompt.text: _____ you use to live near here?

questions.explanation: To form a question with "used to," we use "Did + subject + use to + base verb."

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: After "used to," we use the base form of the verb without "to" or "-ing."

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: When I was younger, I would walk to school every day.
When I was younger, I would be very tall.
When I was younger, I would have a bicycle.
When I was younger, I would know the answer.
questions.answer: When I was younger, I would walk to school every day.
questions.explanation: "Would" is used for repeated past actions (habits), not for past states like "be," "have," or "know."

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: "Although" must be followed by a full clause (subject + verb). "He was sick" is correct.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: "In spite of" is followed by a noun or a gerund. "Feeling tired" is a gerund phrase.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.
questions.explanation: "Questions" is plural, so we use "There are" with plural nouns.

questions.question: Choose the correct plural form.
questions.explanation: "Child" is an irregular noun. Its plural form is "children," not "childs."

questions.question: Choose the correct plural form.
questions.explanation: Some nouns ending in -f change to -ves in the plural form, like "leaf" → "leaves."

questions.question: Choose the correct article.
questions.explanation: We use "the" when both the speaker and listener know which specific thing we are talking about.

questions.question: Choose the correct completion.

prompt.text: She will send the email as soon as she

questions.explanation: After "as soon as," we use the present simple, even when talking about the future.

questions.arabicHint: Even though vs In spite of
questions.question: Choose the correct completion.

prompt.text: _____ the heavy rain, the event continued.

questions.explanation: "In spite of" is followed by a noun phrase (the heavy rain). "Although" and "Even though" must be followed by a full clause.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb form after the preposition.
questions.explanation: After a preposition like "for," we use a gerund (verb + ing). So we say "for helping."

questions.explanation: After "used to," we use the base form of the verb without "-ing" or past tense.

questions.explanation: "Even though" must be followed by a complete clause (subject + verb).

questions.explanation: "People" is a countable plural noun, so we use "many."

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: I didn’t use to like coffee.
I didn’t used to like coffee.
I don’t used to like coffee.
I not use to like coffee.
questions.answer: I didn’t use to like coffee.
questions.explanation: In negative form, we say "didn’t use to + base verb." We do not use "used" after "didn’t."

questions.arabicHint: Although + جملة / In spite of + اسم أو gerund
questions.question: Choose the correct pair to complete the sentence.

prompt.text: _____ he was very nervous, he gave a confident presentation.

options: In spite of / In spite of
questions.explanation: "Although" is followed by a full clause (he was very nervous). "In spite of" is followed by a noun phrase (his fear). The structure determines the correct choice.

questions.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: I will text you as soon as I finish my homework.
I will text you as soon as I will finish my homework.
I text you as soon as I will finish my homework.
I will text you as soon as I finished my homework.
questions.answer: I will text you as soon as I finish my homework.
questions.explanation: After time expressions like "as soon as," we use the present simple (finish), even when referring to the future. We do not use "will" in that clause.

questions.arabicHint: would للعادات الماضية فقط وليس للحالات
questions.question: Choose the correct completion.

prompt.text: When my grandfather was young, he would
stories to his friends every evening.

questions.explanation: "Would" is used for repeated past actions (habits). "Tell stories" is a repeated action. We cannot use "would" for past states like "be" or "have."

questions.arabicHint: There is / There are حسب الاسم الأول
questions.question: Choose the correct completion.

prompt.text: a book and two pens on the table.

questions.explanation: When we use "There is/are" with more than one noun, the verb agrees with the first noun. "A book" is singular, so we use "There is."

questions.arabicHint: the للأشياء الفريدة أو المعروفة + جمع غير منتظم
questions.question: Choose the correct sentence.
options: The children are playing in the yard.
The childs are playing in the yard.
A children are playing in the yard.
questions.answer: The children are playing in the yard.
questions.explanation: "Children" is the correct irregular plural of "child." We also use "the" when referring to specific children and a specific yard known to the speaker and listener.
